meta.title: 4.1 Fetching Data
meta.description: The most common side effect: loading data from a server.

blocks.prompt: When you visit a social media profile, the page shows a spinner for a moment and then your posts appear. What do you think is happening during that spinner? The data is not baked into the HTML -- it comes from somewhere else. How do you think a React component asks a server for data and then displays it?

blocks.question: If a data-fetching component has `loading`, `error`, and `data` state but only checks `if (loading)` before rendering the data, what happens when the fetch fails?
blocks.answer: The component will try to render the data even though it is null (since the fetch failed and data was never set). This typically causes a crash -- something like "Cannot read property 'name' of null." The fix is to also check the error state before attempting to render the data. The guard clause pattern -- `if (loading) return spinner; if (error) return error message; return data;` -- prevents this.

options: A) First: "Loading teams..." text. After data arrives: a list of team names.
B) First: an empty list. After data arrives: team names appear.
C) First: the component crashes. After data: it works.
D) The component always shows "Loading teams..." because the effect never completes.
problems.explanation: On first render, `loading` is `true`, so the component returns the "Loading teams..." paragraph. The useEffect runs after render, fetches from `/api/teams`, and when data arrives, sets `teams` and `loading` to false. The component re-renders, now showing the list of team names. This is the standard tri-state pattern (though this example omits error handling).

options: A) The effect does not reset `loading` to `true` when departmentId changes. The old employees stay visible while the new fetch is in progress.
B) The dependency array should be empty.
C) The employees array should be initialized with null instead of an empty array.
D) The key prop should be the index instead of emp.id.
problems.explanation: When `departmentId` changes, the effect re-runs. But `loading` is only set to `true` on the very first render (the initial state). On subsequent department switches, `loading` is already `false`, so the component continues showing the old employee list until the new data arrives. The fix: add `setLoading(true)` at the start of the effect, before the fetch. This ensures the spinner shows during every re-fetch.

options: A) `loading` starts as `false`, so the spinner never shows. And `!metrics` is `true` (since it starts as `null`), so the component returns `null` -- a blank screen. The fix: initialize `loading` to `true`, and add error handling.
B) The async function syntax is wrong.
C) useEffect's dependency array should include metrics.
D) setMetrics should be called before setLoading.
problems.explanation: Two problems: (1) `loading` starts as `false`. On the first render, the spinner guard clause is skipped. The `!metrics` check returns `null` (blank screen). The fetch is running in the background, but the user sees nothing. (2) There is no error handling -- if the fetch fails, the page stays blank forever. The fix: change `useState(false)` to `useState(true)` so the spinner shows immediately, and wrap the fetch in try/catch to handle errors. You would tell the AI: "Initialize loading to true so the spinner shows on first render, and add try/catch error handling to the fetch."

blocks.prompt: Describe the tri-state pattern for data fetching in React: what three states does every data-fetching component manage? Walk through what happens from the moment the component appears on screen to when the data is displayed.

blocks.content: Data fetching in React follows a universal pattern: useState for data + loading + error, useEffect with fetch, and guard clauses for rendering. The tri-state flow (loading, error, success) appears in almost every component that talks to a server. When reviewing code, check that loading starts as true, errors are handled, and the dependency array is correct.

blocks.prompt: Every loading spinner you see in a web app is backed by this pattern. Next time a page takes a moment to load, picture the three states flipping: loading true, then loading false + data set (success), or loading false + error set (failure). You now understand what is happening under the hood.

reviewQuestions.question: What are the three states every data-fetching component manages?
options: Loading, Error, Success
Open, Closed, Pending
Fetching, Parsing, Rendering
reviewQuestions.explanation: Every data-fetching component manages three states: Loading (waiting for the server), Error (something went wrong), and Success (data is ready to display).
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly right! This tri-state pattern is the foundation of data fetching in React.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Not quite -- the three states are Loading, Error, and Success. You will see this pattern in almost every component that talks to a server.

reviewQuestions.question: What do guard clauses like 'if (loading) return <Spinner />' do in a data-fetching component?
options: They prevent the component from rendering at all
They handle loading and error states first so the main return can safely assume data exists
They skip the useEffect call
reviewQuestions.explanation: Guard clauses (early returns) handle the loading and error states first, so by the time the code reaches the main return statement, it is safe to assume the data is available.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Well done! Guard clauses keep the main rendering logic clean and safe.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Close -- guard clauses handle loading and error states with early returns, so the final return statement can safely access the data without null checks.

reviewQuestions.question: What happens if a useEffect with a fetch call has no dependency array at all?
options: The fetch runs only once on mount
The fetch runs after every render, potentially causing an infinite loop
The fetch never runs
reviewQuestions.explanation: Without a dependency array, useEffect runs after every render. Since the fetch sets state (triggering a re-render), this creates an infinite loop of fetching.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Spot on! A missing dependency array is one of the most common data-fetching mistakes.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Actually, without a dependency array, useEffect runs on every render -- and since fetching sets state, that triggers another render, creating an infinite loop.
